lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia – Pittsfield Municipal Airport (Massachusetts)
Wikipedia – Pittsfield Municipal Airport (Massachusetts)

lostDestinations.carrier: Northeast Airlines (DC-3 service, stopped at PSF 1953–1962)
lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia – Pittsfield Municipal Airport (Massachusetts)

detail: Mohawk DC-3s served Pittsfield 1953–1958 and Northeast DC-3s 1953–1962; commuter carriers Command Airways and Precision Airlines continued until ~1983–84, after which the airport reverted to pure general aviation with no scheduled service since.

lastCarrier: Cape Air (current; Nantucket and Martha's Vineyard only)

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia – New Bedford Regional Airport; New England Aviation History search results

lostDestinations.carrier: Cape Air (discontinued November 7, 2025 due to FAA traffic reduction mandate)
lostDestinations.source: WPRI.com / Cape Air press release reported by newbedford-ma.gov
Wikipedia – New Bedford Regional Airport search summary

detail: Provincetown-Boston Airlines (PBA) was the primary carrier at New Bedford from the early 1980s until 1989 when it ceased all flights, ending decades of mainline scheduled service including three daily nonstop LaGuardia flights with Embraer EMB-110 Bandeirante turboprops; Cape Air subsequently restored island-hopper service but ended its Boston route in November 2025.

lastCarrier: JetBlue / American Airlines / Delta Air Lines (current)

lostDestinations.carrier: Various (Piedmont, Continental, USAir flew mainline jets through early 1990s)
lostDestinations.source: Worcester Regional Research Bureau, Bureau Brief – Worcester Regional Airport, February 2016
Worcester Regional Research Bureau, Bureau Brief – Worcester Regional Airport, February 2016

detail: Worcester peaked at 354,000 enplanements in 1989 when Piedmont, Northwest, Continental, and USAir flew mainline jets to the airport, but by the late 1990s annual enplanements had fallen below 10,000 as all major carriers departed; Massport assumed operations in 2000, and JetBlue restored service in November 2013 after a roughly 14-year absence of meaningful scheduled flights.
source: Worcester Regional Research Bureau Bureau Brief (February 2016); https://www.wrrb.org/reports/2016/02/bureau-brief-worcester-regional-airport-orh/

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia – Sikorsky Memorial Airport; Alchetron – Sikorsky Memorial Airport
Wikipedia – Sikorsky Memorial Airport search results
Wikipedia – Sikorsky Memorial Airport search results
Wikipedia – Sikorsky Memorial Airport; airport served Washington DC, Philadelphia, Boston and Newark in 1992

detail: At its peak in the 1980s Bridgeport was served by five carriers including Business Express, Continental, Piedmont, US Air, and United Express; Continental Connection departed in 1994, Delta Connection in 1997, and US Airways Express ended the last scheduled flights in November 1999, leaving BDR without any commercial service for over 25 years.

lostDestinations.toName: New York John F. Kennedy International
lostDestinations.carrier: Pilgrim Airlines (founded 1962 specifically to link Groton with JFK)
lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia – Groton–New London Airport search results

lostDestinations.carrier: Various (Pilgrim, Pan Am Clipper Connection, Piedmont served Boston)
lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia – Groton–New London Airport search results
Wikipedia – Groton–New London Airport search results

detail: Pilgrim Airlines was founded in 1962 specifically to link Groton with JFK; after Pilgrim's absorption and a string of single-carrier operations (Pan Am Clipper Connection, Piedmont, Allegheny), US Airways Express operated the last scheduled route—GON–PHL—until it terminated service in 2004, and the airport has had no scheduled commercial flights since despite continuing to hold FAA commercial certification.

lastCarrier: Breeze Airways (current, replacing Spirit Airlines May 2026)

lostDestinations.source: Wikipedia – Atlantic City International Airport search results; Atlantic City airport history
Wikipedia – Atlantic City International Airport search results

lostDestinations.carrier: Air Canada (seasonal summer 2015, did not return in 2016)
lostDestinations.source: Atlantic City International Airport history; acairport.com/acy-history search results
Atlantic City International Airport Wikipedia article search results
Atlantic City International Airport Wikipedia article search results

detail: After United Airlines ended its Chicago O'Hare and Houston routes on December 3, 2014, and Air Canada dropped its seasonal Toronto service after summer 2015, Spirit Airlines remained the sole scheduled carrier at ACY for roughly a decade, accounting for ~75% of flights at its peak, before filing for bankruptcy and ceasing all operations in May 2026; Breeze Airways has begun restoring routes.
source: Wikipedia – Atlantic City International Airport; WHYY 'Atlantic City airport finds new carriers for lost Spirit flights' (2026); https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atlantic_City_International_Airport
